query: Summarise the requirements for access control

trace.question: Summarise the requirements for access control

plan.original_query: Summarise the requirements for access control
plan.interpretation: The user wants a concise summary of the NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 requirements related to access control (the Access Control family and its controls).

steps.rationale: Retrieve all NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 controls in the Access Control (AC) family so the key requirements (policy, account management, least privilege, separation of duties, access enforcement, etc.) can be summarized.

plan.original_query: Summarise the requirements for access control
plan.interpretation: The user wants a concise summary of the NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 requirements related to access control (the Access Control family and its controls).

steps.rationale: Retrieve all NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 controls in the Access Control (AC) family so the key requirements (policy, account management, least privilege, separation of duties, access enforcement, etc.) can be summarized.

documents.title: Access Enforcement — Attribute-based Access Control
documents.content:
Enforce attribute-based access control policy over defined subjects and objects and control access based upon [Assignment: attributes].

Discussion: Attribute-based access control is an access control policy that restricts system access to authorized users based on specified organizational attributes (e.g., job function, identity), action attributes (e.g., read, write, delete), environmental attributes (e.g., time of day, location), and resource attributes (e.g., classification of a document). Organizations can create rules based on attributes and the authorizations (i.e., privileges) to perform needed operations on the systems associated with organization-defined attributes and rules. When users are assigned to attributes defined in attribute-based access control policies or rules, they can be provisioned to a system with the appropriate privileges or dynamically granted access to a protected resource. Attribute-based access control can be implemented as either a mandatory or discretionary form of access control. When implemented with mandatory access controls, the requirements in [AC-3(3)](#ac-3.3) define the scope of the subjects and objects covered by the policy.

documents.title: Access Enforcement — Discretionary Access Control
Access Enforcement — Discretionary and Mandatory Access Control
documents.content:
(a) Enforce [Assignment: organization-defined mandatory access control policy] over the set of covered subjects and objects specified in the policy; and
(b) Enforce [Assignment: organization-defined discretionary access control policy] over the set of covered subjects and objects specified in the policy.

Discussion: Simultaneously implementing a mandatory access control policy and a discretionary access control policy can provide additional protection against the unauthorized execution of code by users or processes acting on behalf of users. This helps prevent a single compromised user or process from compromising the entire system.

documents.title: Access Enforcement — Role-based Access Control
documents.content:
Enforce a role-based access control policy over defined subjects and objects and control access based upon [Assignment: organization-defined roles and users authorized to assume such roles].

Discussion: Role-based access control (RBAC) is an access control policy that enforces access to objects and system functions based on the defined role (i.e., job function) of the subject. Organizations can create specific roles based on job functions and the authorizations (i.e., privileges) to perform needed operations on the systems associated with the organization-defined roles. When users are assigned to specific roles, they inherit the authorizations or privileges defined for those roles. RBAC simplifies privilege administration for organizations because privileges are not assigned directly to every user (which can be a large number of individuals) but are instead acquired through role assignments. RBAC can also increase privacy and security risk if individuals assigned to a role are given access to information beyond what they need to support organizational missions or business functions. RBAC can be implemented as a mandatory or discretionary form of access control. For organizations implementing RBAC with mandatory access controls, the requirements in [AC-3(3)](#ac-3.3) define the scope of the subjects and objects covered by the policy.

documents.title: Access Enforcement — Mandatory Access Control

answer.answer:
Summary of access control requirements:

- General requirement: enforce an organization-defined access control policy over specified subjects and objects; the policy type is chosen by the organization (mandatory, discretionary, role-based, attribute-based, or combinations). (AC-3(3), AC-3(4), AC-3(7), AC-3(13), AC-3(15))

- Mandatory Access Control (MAC): the MAC policy must be uniformly enforced across covered subjects/objects; subjects with access are constrained from (1) passing information to unauthorized subjects/objects, (2) granting privileges to others, (3) changing specified security attributes, (4) choosing security attributes/values for new or modified objects, and (5) changing access-control rules. The policy is bounded by the system, and exceptions can be made by explicitly granting privileges to specified subjects. Trusted subjects receive privileges consistent with least privilege. (AC-3(3))

- Discretionary Access Control (DAC): where DAC is used, enforce the organization-defined DAC policy over covered subjects/objects; DAC permits subjects that have been granted access to (a) pass the information to other subjects/objects, (b) grant their privileges to others, (c) change security attributes, (d) choose attributes for newly created/revised objects, and (e) change access-control rules. DAC is bounded by the system and can operate alongside MAC. (AC-3(4), AC-3(3))

- Role-Based Access Control (RBAC): enforce an RBAC policy that controls access based on organization-defined roles and authorized users for those roles; users inherit the privileges of assigned roles. RBAC can be implemented as mandatory or discretionary and may be combined with MAC constraints. (AC-3(7), AC-3(3), AC-3(4))

- Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC): enforce ABAC rules that grant or deny access based on attributes (e.g., subject/job function, action, environment such as time/location, and resource classification); rules can provision or dynamically grant appropriate privileges. ABAC can be mandatory or discretionary. (AC-3(13))

- Combined enforcement: systems may be required to enforce both mandatory and discretionary policies simultaneously to add protection (e.g., limit damage from a single compromised subject). (AC-3(15))

- Operational note: access-control policies are effective only within system boundaries; once information leaves system control additional protections may be needed. (AC-3(3), AC-3(4))

llm_judge.justification: All retrieved AC-3 control enhancements (MAC, DAC, RBAC, ABAC, and combined enforcement) directly address access-control requirements and each claim in the summary can be traced to the corresponding cited controls (AC-3(3), AC-3(4), AC-3(7), AC-3(13), AC-3(15)).
